title: DAVID — The Language That Rewrote the Family Tree

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Hittite — the Bronze Age language of Anatolia, written in cuneiform on thirty thousand clay tablets, that forced linguists to redraw the entire Indo-European family tree.

Linguists had reconstructed Proto-Indo-European without a single written specimen. Then in 1906 archaeologists excavated Hattusa — modern Turkey — and found a language no one expected to be Indo-European at all.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is in the cuneiform archive: the Treaty of Kadesh, the oldest surviving peace treaty, written in both Hittite and Akkadian — and in the laryngeal consonants that Saussure predicted in Proto-Indo-European decades before Hittite was even deciphered.

We reconstruct Hittite corpus-first: the cuneiform syllabary records consonants and vowels directly, comparative Indo-European fills the gaps, and the Anitta text — the oldest Hittite inscription — anchors the earliest phonology.
The syllabary fixes much of the consonant inventory, but Hittite orthography merged several sounds the writing did not distinguish, and pitch accent remains debated. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — nu GIM-an dUTU-uš nepišaz andan uizzi — as the sun god comes in from heaven, the ceremonial prayer that opens a Hittite ritual tablet from Hattusa.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Hittite · KBo 17.74 ritual prayer

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
